# Questions in Livestream 15

Live Stream #15: And Other Taboo Jewish Numbers

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=339" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:39</a> | How does the *Instruction of Merikare* saying that humans are "the likeness of god" relate to Genesis 1:26? | He said the Egyptian text is not the same doctrine as the image of God, though both can speak about humans becoming god-like or wielding god-like power. | He says the Merikare wording is interesting but should not be flattened into Genesis 1. In Egyptian context it can describe humans exercising god-like authority or becoming like a god in some respect, while Genesis uses image language within its own creation theology and human vocation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:32</a> | Would Egyptian contextual understanding still have shaped Jews in Jesus's day? | Only faintly. He said the original Egyptian background had largely dropped out by the first century, even though core traditions like Passover remained. | He argues that by the first century many older Egyptian resonances would no longer be consciously active for most Jewish readers. Traditions such as Passover still preserved Egypt as a core memory, but the detailed Egyptian cultural setting of the Exodus had largely receded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:10</a> | Which active archaeological site looks most promising, and which site is being overworked? | He highlighted places like Tell el-Retaba and Carchemish as promising, and criticized the continued excavation of Shiloh as beating a dead horse. | He points to sites that can still clarify major historical problems, including Tell el-Retaba for Egypt-and-Exodus-related questions and Carchemish for broader Near Eastern history. By contrast, he thinks Shiloh has already been pushed too hard relative to what it can still yield. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=1225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:25</a> | How much Egyptian culture remained after Hellenization? | He said Hellenization was strong, especially in the north, but Egyptian culture also resisted it and continued alongside Greek culture. | He describes Ptolemaic and later Egypt as culturally layered rather than simply replaced by Greek culture. Greek influence was strongest in places like Alexandria and the Delta, while Egyptian language, religion, temples, and local identity continued, especially farther south. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=1451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:11</a> | Is there a published original-language edition of the Dead Sea Scrolls? | Yes. He recommended the *Discoveries in the Judaean Desert* series, while noting that it is excellent but extremely expensive. | He points to the *Discoveries in the Judaean Desert* volumes as the major scholarly publication series for the scrolls in the original languages. His practical caveat is cost: it is the kind of resource libraries or specialists use, not an easy personal purchase. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=1668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:48</a> | Were there human sacrifices in ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia, and when did they end? | Yes, in both. He cited royal retainers, prisoners of war, and possible foundation deposits, but said the evidence is limited and did not give a firm end date. | He distinguishes several categories of evidence: retainer sacrifice around early royal burials, killing prisoners of war, and possible ritual foundation contexts. He does not give a clean end date because the evidence is uneven and the practices were not all the same kind of sacrifice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=1804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:04</a> | Are the Dead Sea Scrolls basically the same as the Bible we have today? | Broadly yes at the doctrinal level, but they preserve meaningful textual variants and show multiple textual traditions. | He says the scrolls confirm substantial continuity with later biblical texts, but they also show that the textual tradition was not monolithic. Some variants are minor, while others reveal different textual families behind books that later became standardized. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=1984" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:04</a> | What books does he recommend on Egyptian or ancient Near Eastern chronology? | He chiefly recommended *Ancient Egyptian Chronology* edited by Erik Hornung, Rolf Krauss, and David Warburton. | His main recommendation is the Hornung, Krauss, and Warburton volume because it gathers specialist discussions of Egyptian chronological problems. The answer points users toward technical chronology work rather than popular summaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=2190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:30</a> | What path does he recommend for advanced study in Old Testament, New Testament, or patristics, and should it be online? | He strongly advised in-person study, warned against trying to master all three tracks at once, and said graduate training depends heavily on relationships and departmental fit. | He advises narrowing the field first, because Old Testament, New Testament, and patristics each require different languages and scholarly networks. He strongly favors in-person graduate work because mentorship, seminars, and relationships with faculty shape the training as much as course content. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=2540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:20</a> | Is Redford recent enough to count as current scholarship, and should pessimistic induction be applied to the current field? | He said speculative theories can become dated quickly, while primary data and transcriptions age better; scholars need to keep reading and test claims against current evidence. | He distinguishes durable scholarly work from interpretations that age quickly. Editions, transcriptions, and primary data can remain useful for decades, but reconstructions and speculative historical theories need to be checked against newer finds and current debates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=2940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:00</a> | For Old Testament doctoral work, should the focus be the university or the supervisor? | The supervisor. He said students should identify the scholar they want to study under first, then evaluate the department and institution. | He says doctoral study is built around the advisor relationship, so the student's primary question should be who can supervise the specific research they want to do. Institution and department still matter, but they come after finding the right scholar and scholarly fit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=3337" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:37</a> | Why does he call Ron Wyatt a charlatan? | Because Wyatt made sensational claims without real evidence, and Falk argued that such stories can badly damage people's faith when they collapse. | He criticizes Wyatt for making dramatic archaeological claims without the evidence needed to sustain them. His concern is pastoral as well as scholarly: when people build faith on spectacular but false claims, the collapse of those claims can cause avoidable damage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=3690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:30</a> | What does he think of the Hobby Lobby antiquities debacle? | He said the Museum of the Bible was a worthwhile goal pursued badly; the acquisitions should have been handled lawfully and more carefully. | He separates the desire to build a Bible museum from the way the artifacts were acquired. The goal could have been valuable, but provenance, legal importation, and professional handling matter, and ignoring those responsibilities damaged the project. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=3958" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:58</a> | Why does modern biblical studies often seem more speculative than Egyptology or Assyriology? | He said biblical studies leans more heavily on secondary literature and inherited academic tradition, while the other fields are often more tightly tied to primary data. | He argues that Egyptology and Assyriology often force scholars back to inscriptions, objects, and primary languages, while biblical studies can drift into secondary-theory conversations. The difference is not that biblical studies lacks evidence, but that its academic habits can reward speculation more readily. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=4359" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:39</a> | Is online undergraduate work acceptable if someone hopes to do in-person postgraduate study later? | He said it can be done, but it still weakens later preparation and admissions compared with doing as much as possible in person. | He does not say online study makes graduate work impossible, but he warns that it usually gives weaker preparation, fewer relationships, and less visible faculty support. His advice is to do as much serious in-person language and subject training as possible before applying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=4750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:10</a> | Which ancient Egyptian temple is his favorite? | He chose the Temple of Seti I at Abydos, with Luxor and Dendera also receiving praise. | He names the Seti I temple at Abydos as his favorite because of its preservation, decoration, and historical interest. Luxor and Dendera also stand out to him, but Abydos gets the top spot in this answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=5052" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:12</a> | Can he break down the Egyptian word *heka* and its usage, and clarify the seated-god determinative? | He said *heka* can refer to magic in several forms, while determinatives are unpronounced signs; the seated-god determinative is not *heka* and is mostly used with male deities or divinized dead kings. | He explains *heka* as a word for magic or magical power, while also clarifying how hieroglyphic determinatives work. The seated-god sign is not pronounced as *heka*; it marks a divine name or divinized person and usually appears with male gods or dead kings treated as divine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=5487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:27</a> | Can we know whether the Jerubbaal inscription refers to Gideon? | No. He said the identification is possible and even plausible, but still remains speculative. | He says the name makes the connection tempting, and it is not impossible that the inscription belongs in a context related to the biblical Gideon tradition. But the evidence cannot identify the person securely, so the responsible conclusion is possibility rather than proof. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=5680" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:40</a> | Does he choose non-super-chat questions from the top, the bottom, or by another method? | He said he does not have a fixed method; he jumps around and chooses non-super-chat questions that look interesting. | He describes the selection process as informal. Super chats get priority, but for ordinary chat questions he scans around and chooses ones that catch his attention or seem worth answering rather than following a strict top-to-bottom order. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=5762" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:02</a> | Can Joseph's time as vizier be inferred from the transfer of land to the king in Egypt, and did that become permanent? | He said that pattern fits the Hyksos period well and points to Joseph in the Second Intermediate Period, though later landholding became more mixed. | He connects Genesis's description of land transfer to conditions that make better sense in the Hyksos or Second Intermediate Period than in later centralized arrangements. He also notes that landholding did not remain a single simple royal monopoly forever; later periods show more mixed patterns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=6049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:49</a> | Did ordinary Egyptians move around a lot, or stay in the same house for life? | He said poorer farming families often shifted between high ground and floodplain seasonally, while elites could remain more settled. | He distinguishes social classes and ecological settings. Poorer agricultural families could move between safer high ground and fields near the inundation cycle, while elites with more permanent houses, estates, or administrative posts could be more stable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=6208" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:28</a> | What are his thoughts on *Neon Genesis Evangelion* and favorite anime more broadly? | He said Gundam is his clear favorite, while *Evangelion* never connected with him in the same way. | He answers as a fan rather than a scholar: *Evangelion* did not resonate strongly with him, while Gundam is his clear favorite franchise. The answer is mostly about personal taste, not an extended interpretation of anime symbolism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=6446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:26</a> | How did kingship develop as an institution? | He linked it to settled agrarian society, irrigation management, storage, and the need to protect the food supply. | He describes kingship as emerging from practical pressures in settled agricultural societies. Irrigation, surplus storage, redistribution, defense, and dispute control create a need for centralized authority that can eventually become royal power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/s3grFd_g6yk?t=6727" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:07</a> | What are the earliest Egyptian writings that discuss Greece? | He said Egypt recognized Greece by the New Kingdom, but Egyptian literature was generally not very interested in detailed foreign ethnography. | He says Egyptians knew of Aegean or Greek peoples by the New Kingdom, but Egyptian texts usually mention foreigners in limited administrative, diplomatic, or ideological ways. They do not typically provide the kind of detailed ethnographic discussion a modern reader might want. |
